lessons.title: First Steps with Python
lessons.description: Write a program to display 'Hello, World!'

lessons.title: Variables and Calculations
lessons.description:
Write a program that assigns your age to a variable, calculates your age in 10 years, and displays it.
For example, if you're 14 now, you'll be 24 in 10 years.

lessons.title: Data Types and Conversion
lessons.description:
Write a program that converts two numbers given as strings to integers, calculates their sum, and displays it.
Finally, display the result in the format "The sum is ○○".

lessons.title: User Input
lessons.description:
Use the input() function to ask the user for their name and return a greeting.
Display the greeting in the format "Hello, ○○!"

lessons.title: Debugging Basics
lessons.description:
The following code has 3 bugs. Read the error messages and fix all the bugs.
After fixing them, verify that it works correctly.
Expected behavior:

  Display "Taro-kun will be 16 years old next year"
  Display "Height is 160cm, and if it grows 10cm, it will be 170cm"

chapters.title: Conditional Statements
chapters.description: Learn to control program flow with conditions

lessons.title: Basics of Conditional Statements
lessons.description:
Create a program that asks the user to enter a number, then determines if it's positive, negative, or zero.
Examples:

  Input: 5 → Output: Positive number
  Input: -3 → Output: Negative number
  Input: 0 → Output: Zero

lessons.title: else and elif
lessons.description:
Complete a rock-paper-scissors game program.
Create a program that plays rock-paper-scissors with the computer and determines the winner.
Game Rules

  Rock (1) beats Scissors (2)
  Scissors (2) beats Paper (3)
  Paper (3) beats Rock (1)
  Same choice results in a tie

lessons.title: Logical Operators
lessons.description:
Let's create a program to find who can ride the attraction among 4 people at a theme park.
The attraction requirements are: "6 years or older" AND "120cm or taller".
Data for 4 People

  A: 8 years old, 125cm
  B: 5 years old, 130cm
  C: 10 years old, 115cm
  D: 4 years old, 140cm

Print the name (A, B, C, or D) of the person who meets the requirements.

lessons.title: for Loop Basics
lessons.description:
Implement the function print_multiplication_table(n) that prints a multiplication table.
It displays the n-times table from 1 to 9.
Output Format
For example, calling print_multiplication_table(3) should produce:
3 × 1 = 3
3 × 2 = 6
3 × 3 = 9
3 × 4 = 12
3 × 5 = 15
3 × 6 = 18
3 × 7 = 21
3 × 8 = 24
3 × 9 = 27

lessons.title: Advanced for Loops
lessons.description:
Implement the function sum_numbers(n).
This function returns the sum of all integers from 1 to n.
Example Output
For example, calling sum_numbers(5) computes:
  1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 15
so it returns 15.
sum_numbers(10) returns 55, and sum_numbers(100) returns 5050.

lessons.title: while Loops
lessons.description:
Implement the function check_guess(secret, guess).
This function returns a hint for a number guessing game.

  If guess is less than secret → return "Too low"
  If guess is greater than secret → return "Too high"
  If guess equals secret → return "Correct"

Example
  check_guess(50, 30)  → "Too low"
check_guess(50, 70)  → "Too high"
check_guess(50, 50)  → "Correct"

chapters.title: Data Structures
chapters.description: Learn to work with collections of data

lessons.title: List Basics
lessons.description:
Implement the function get_element(fruits, index).
fruits: a list of fruit name strings
index: the zero-based index of the element to retrieve
Return the element at the given index. If the index is out of range, return None.
Examples
  get_element(["apple", "banana", "orange"], 0)  → "apple"
get_element(["apple", "banana", "orange"], 2)  → "orange"
get_element(["apple", "banana"], 5)            → None (out of range)

lessons.title: List Operations
lessons.description:
Implement the function find_max(numbers).
numbers: a non-empty list of numbers
Return the maximum value in the list. Do not use the built-in max() function — instead, use a for loop to compare all elements.
Examples
  find_max([3, 7, 2, 9, 1])    → 9
find_max([5])                → 5
find_max([-5, -2, -10, -1]) → -1

lessons.title: for Loops and Lists
lessons.description:
Implement the function filter_even(numbers).
It receives a list of integers numbers and returns a new list containing only the even numbers.
Examples
  filter_even([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6])  # [2, 4, 6]
filter_even([1, 3, 5])            # []
filter_even([10, 15, 20, 25])     # [10, 20]

lessons.title: Dictionary Basics
lessons.description:
Implement the function get_score(students, name).
students is a dictionary with student names as keys and scores as values. Return the score for the student specified by name.
If the student does not exist in the dictionary, return -1.
Examples
  students = {"Alice": 85, "Bob": 92, "Charlie": 78}
get_score(students, "Alice")    # 85
get_score(students, "Bob")      # 92
get_score(students, "Dave")     # -1 (not found)

lessons.title: Function Basics
lessons.description:
Implement the function add(a, b).
The function receives two numbers a and b, and returns their sum using a return statement.
Example Output
  print(add(3, 5))    # 8
print(add(10, 20))  # 30
print(add(-5, 3))   # -2

lessons.title: Advanced Functions
lessons.description:
Implement the function calculate(a, b, operation="add").
It receives two numbers a and b, and an operation type operation, then returns the result of the specified operation.
Operation Types

  "add": return a + b (default)
  "subtract": return a - b
  "multiply": return a * b
  "divide": return a / b (return None if b is 0)

Example Output
  print(calculate(10, 5))                  # 15 (default is addition)
print(calculate(10, 5, "subtract"))      # 5
print(calculate(10, 5, "multiply"))      # 50
print(calculate(10, 5, "divide"))        # 2.0
print(calculate(10, 0, "divide"))        # None

chapters.title: Practical Projects
chapters.description: Apply your knowledge to build real programs

lessons.title: Text-Based Game
lessons.description:
Let's implement the two core functions of a number guessing game.
Functions to Implement

  check_guess(secret, guess): A function that judges a guess
  play_round(secret, guesses): A function that processes a list of guesses

Specification for check_guess

  If guess is less than secret, return "Too low"
  If guess is greater than secret, return "Too high"
  If guess equals secret, return "Correct"

Specification for play_round

  guesses: a list of guesses (e.g., [30, 70, 50])
  Check each guess in order using check_guess
  When "Correct" is found, return a tuple (number of attempts, "Correct")
  If all guesses are wrong, return (length of guesses, last judgment result)

lessons.title: Data Analysis Program
lessons.description:
Let's implement three functions that analyze student grade data.
Functions to Implement

  calculate_average(scores): Calculate the average score for each student
  find_top_student(averages): Return the name of the student with the highest average
  count_passing(averages, passing_score=60): Return the number of passing students

Specification for calculate_average

  Argument scores: in the format {"Alice": [85, 90, 88], ...}
  Return value: in the format {"Alice": 87.666..., ...} (average for each student)
  Use sum() and len() to calculate the average

Specification for find_top_student

  Argument averages: in the format {"Alice": 87.67, ...}
  Return value: the name (string) of the student with the highest average

Specification for count_passing

  Argument averages: in the format {"Alice": 87.67, ...}
  Argument passing_score: the passing threshold (default 60)
  Return value: the number of students with averages at or above the passing score (integer)

lessons.title: File Operations Basics
lessons.description:
As a basic feature of a memo app, implement two functions to save and load text files.

  save_memo(filename, content): A function that saves text to a file
  load_memo(filename): A function that reads text from a file

lessons.title: Final Project: Task Manager App
lessons.description:
Let's complete the task manager app! Implement the following 5 functions.

  add_task(tasks, task_name): Add a task to the list and return it
  complete_task(tasks, task_id): Mark the task with the given id as complete and return the list
  show_tasks(tasks): Display all tasks
  save_tasks(tasks, filename): Save tasks to a JSON file
  load_tasks(filename): Load tasks from a JSON file

Task Data Structure
Each task is a dictionary in this format: {"id": 1, "name": "Task name", "completed": False}
